title: Super-Link Fragility in Asymmetric W-Class States under Quantum Noise
authors: Sougata Bhattacharyya, Fatih Ozaydin, Sovik Roy
abstract: The asymmetric three-qubit W-class state $|\overline{W_3^L}\rangle$ defines an isosceles entanglement-network geometry, (a) two vertex-base (VB) links form stronger bipartite connections, (b) while the base-base (BB) link is weaker. This suggests that concentrating entanglement into a super-link may be advantageous for quantum-network tasks. Here, we show that this intuition is incomplete. We analytically compare the bipartite concurrence dynamics of the symmetric |W> state and the asymmetric $|\overline{W_3^L}\rangle$ state, which differ both in entanglement-network geometry and excitation sector under standard noise models. In the absence of noise, the concurrence hierarchy is C_{VB} > C_W > C_{BB}$. Under phase damping, this hierarchy is preserved for all noise strengths and no entanglement sudden death occurs. Under amplitude damping, however, the hierarchy is reordered. The symmetric |W> state becomes the most robust, while the base-base concurrence of $|\overline{W_3^L}\rangle$ vanishes at the finite threshold of parameter $γ$. We term this reordering as the \textit{Super-Link Fragility Effect}. The same structural asymmetry that produces a stronger vertex-base link also makes it more vulnerable to energy dissipation when coupled with multi-excitation amplitudes. Under depolarization, the asymmetry advantage is erased, with $C_W$ and $C_{VB}$ sharing the same sudden-death threshold for some value of the parameter p, while $C_{BB}$ disappears earlier at some other value of the parameter p. The generalized amplitude damping channel continuously connects the damping-dominated regime to the pure-excitation limit, where the initial hierarchy is restored. These results show that entanglement robustness in $W$-class resources is controlled not by initial concurrence alone, but by the joint structure of entanglement-network geometry, excitation sector, and noise symmetry.
summary: This paper analyzes the robustness of entanglement in asymmetric three-qubit W-states compared to symmetric ones when exposed to different types of quantum noise. It demonstrates that initial structural advantages, such as a strong 'super-link,' can be fragile and even reverse under specific decoherence channels like amplitude damping. The findings emphasize that entanglement resilience depends on the interplay between network geometry, excitation sector, and noise symmetry.
main_problem: The study investigates how the initial structural advantages of asymmetric W-class quantum states persist or degrade when subjected to various forms of quantum noise.
main_result: The initial entanglement hierarchy ($C_{VB} > C_W > C_{BB}$) is not guaranteed under decoherence; for instance, amplitude damping causes a reversal termed the Super-Link Fragility Effect.
method: The authors analytically compare bipartite concurrence dynamics between symmetric and asymmetric W states using established noise models (phase, amplitude, depolarization).
model_or_system: The system involves three-qubit entangled W-class states ($|Wangle$ vs. $|\overline{W_3^L}angle$), whose entanglement geometry is analyzed via bipartite concurrence links (Vertex-Base and Base-Base).
key_observables: Bipartite Concurrence ($C_{VB}, C_W, C_{BB}$), Entanglement Sudden Death (ESD) thresholds.
important_parameters: Noise parameters ($\gamma$ for amplitude damping, $p$ for depolarization, $\alpha$ for GADC).
main_assumptions: The analysis often relies on the assumption that reduced density matrices remain X-states to simplify concurrence calculations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate entanglement decay under different noise channels (e.g., Fig 7 shows hierarchy reversal under amplitude damping; Fig 8 shows ESD thresholds under depolarization).
paper_structure: The paper establishes the initial geometric advantage, then systematically analyzes the robustness of this structure across four distinct noise models: phase damping (preserves hierarchy), amplitude damping (reverses hierarchy/Super-Link Fragility), depolarization (erases asymmetry), and GADC (interpolates between limits).
why_interesting: This work provides crucial insights into how structural properties derived from ideal quantum states break down under realistic, non-Markovian noise environments, which is vital for designing robust quantum communication protocols.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Borromean Rings. Three mutually inter- locked rings in which no two rings are directly linked as a pair, yet the three together cannot be separated. This topology serves as an analogy for the Borromean mode of three-qubit entanglement: when any single qubit is measured and found in |0⟩, the residual two-qubit state of the remaining pair collapses to a fully separable prod- uct state with zero coherence (Cl1 = 0), mirroring how severing one Borromean ring causes the other two to fall entirely apart.

FIG. 2: 3-Hopf Link. Three rings arranged so that removing any single ring still leaves the remaining two interlinked. This topology serves as an analogy for the Hopf mode of three-qubit entanglement: when any single qubit is measured and found in |1⟩, the residual two-qubit state retains non-zero coherence (Cl1 > 0) and bipartite entanglement, mirroring the persistent linkage of the re- maining two Hopf rings.

FIG. 3: l1-norm coherence of the two-qubit re- mainder after single-qubit measurement. For each measured qubit (A, B, or C), the two endpoints of the error bar mark the two possible coherence values corre- sponding to the measurement outcomes |0⟩and |1⟩; the bar height marks the probability-weighted average (ex- pectation value) of the residual coherence over the two possible measurement outcomes, and represents the ex- pected coherence remaining after a random single-qubit measurement. Dashed horizontal lines mark the initial coherence of each three-qubit state before any measure- ment: Cinit l1 ≈1.914 for |W L 3 ⟩(blue) and Cinit l1 = 2 for |W⟩ (red). FIG. 5: Isosceles entanglement-network geom- etry of the asymmetric |W L 3 ⟩state. The vertex qubit A (top) acts as a balanced switch: it triggers the Hopf mode with probability 1

FIG. 4: Equilateral entanglement-network ge- ometry of the symmetric |W⟩state. Each vertex represents a qubit (A, B, C) and each edge represents a bipartite entanglement link. Because the single excita- tion is distributed uniformly, all three qubits play struc- turally identical roles. Every qubit acts as an unbiased probabilistic switch, triggering the Hopf mode (residual coherence Cl1 = 1.0) with probability 2 3 and the Bor- romean mode (residual separability, Cl1 = 0) with prob- ability 1 3. Consequently, tracing out any single qubit yields the same reduced density matrix, and all three bipartite concurrences are equal: CW = 2
